In this paper, we make three contributions. First, we provide a new interpretation of latent correlations based on the alignment between within-cluster and between-cluster correlation submatrices. Second, we define a novel alignment ratio and use it to analyze the effects of misspecification. Finally, we show that misspecification, whether through misassignment or misalignment, inflates estimated latent correlations among clusters.


## Consequences of inflated correlations

What are the real-world consequences of inflated correlation estimates? In cognitive research, such high latent correlations are often treated as central evidence, sometimes to the point that manifest correlations receive little attention.^[Manifest correlations are typically reported, but in tabular form that is difficult to visualize.] The interpretation is that latent correlations have substantive meaning in their own right, driven by their large magnitude. In @Tsukahara.etal.2020, for example, the high correlation between attentional control and sensory discrimination is used, in part, to support the conclusion that variation in attentional control causes variation in sensory discrimination.

In more sober presentations, high latent correlations may lead to conclusions that a reduced factor model is adequate, or that clusters lack discriminant validity. Consider the third column of Figure \ref{fig:het-bd}. With $\widehat{\varphi}\approx 0.85$, a common reading is that the clusters have high unity and weak discriminant validity, given the usual 0.85 rule of thumb for factor correlations [@Kline.2011; @Shaffer.etal.2016; @vanMierlo.etal.2009]. Yet this rule of thumb fails here. The generative model is bifactor--the tasks are organized by two cluster-specific factors that are orthogonal to each other and orthogonal to the general factor. The clusters are therefore not redundant, and capturing the covariance structure requires more than a single common factor.

So the natural question is how inference based on a latent correlation can be justified.  The usual justification for any CFA is (a) acceptable global fit of the measurement model [@Bentler.1990; @Hu.Bentler.1999; @Steiger.1990] assessed with indices such as CFI and RMSEA (alongside $\chi^2$), and (b) a targeted nested comparison probing empirical distinguishability [@Bagozzi.etal.1991], for example by fixing the relevant factor correlation to 1 and testing whether fit worsens. In @Tsukahara.etal.2020, these checks, including a correlation-to-unity test, support a "unity" interpretation for attentional control and sensory discrimination.

Clearly, these methods are not adequate for the @Tsukahara.etal.2020 data set.  Panel (B$_3$) of Figure \ref{fig:corPats} shows that the corresponding alignment ratios are highly dispersed, spanning values near 0 and extending above 1. In this context, the population alignment ratios are not invariant and therefore there is no single cluster-level latent correlation that summarizes between-cluster structure. Global fit and unity tests do not diagnose this failure of alignment. This is precisely why alignment-ratio invariance is critical. It is the condition under which $\widehat{\varphi}$ is a well-defined summary of between-cluster structure, and it provides a direct diagnostic for when latent-correlation inference is or is not warranted.  

Alignment ratios and their invariance provide a useful diagnostic for assessing both model validity and the interpretability of latent correlations. If the alignment ratios are not invariant, then there is no single population latent correlation that captures between-cluster relations, and inference based on the fitted latent correlation is not warranted. If the alignment ratios are invariant, then the fitted latent correlation is interpretable as the common alignment ratio, and inference from it is justified. Beyond diagnosis, the alignment-ratio framework clarifies what the model is compressing into $\widehat{\varphi}$. Through the bifactor link, $\varphi$ admits an interpretation in terms of the relative strength of global covariance captured by a general factor versus local covariance captured by cluster-specific factors. When the general factor dominates, fitted latent correlations approach unity. When cluster-specific structure is comparatively strong, fitted latent correlations are lower. This reading is consistent with the use of latent correlations as a measure of discriminant validity, where higher $\varphi$ suggests weaker discriminant validity and lower $\varphi$ suggests stronger discriminant validity.

## Alternatives to ICM-CFA

There are several alternatives to ICM-CFA.  One is to retain a cluster structure but relax the constraint that tasks load on one and only one factor via ESEM [@Asparouhov.Muthen.2009; @Marsh.etal.2014; @Morin.etal.2016]. Allowing cross-cluster loadings reduces the need for the model to absorb cross-cluster covariance into the latent correlation, and it can therefore attenuate the inflation mechanism emphasized in the ESEM literature. However, ESEM trades this benefit for additional flexibility and ambiguity. Factor definitions depend on rotation and target specifications, and the resulting factor correlations may be sensitive to these choices. This flexibility can also weaken the simple cluster-based interpretation that motivates ICM-CFA in cognitive batteries, where factors are intended to map cleanly onto named constructs. For this reason, ESEM has been less widely adopted in cognitive-battery applications, despite its appeal as a remedy for cluster misassignment.  Moreover, permitting cross-loadings directly addresses misassignment-type misspecification, but it does not, by itself, guarantee alignment of within-cluster and between-cluster submatrices.

Bifactor models seem better positioned to address misalignment, since the general factor can capture covariance that is shared across clusters while remaining orthogonal in its structure from the cluster-specific factors in the standard bifactor specification [@Holzinger.Swineford.1937; @Reise.2012]. It may be better for researchers to specify a bifactor model with a general factor and specific factors corresponding to the clusters, rather than an ICM-CFA. If and only if the proportionality constraint holds, then a sound latent correlation between two clusters can be estimated from the bifactor model \eqref{bifaclatcor}. For cases with more than two clusters, there must be rank-one structure among the latent correlations and, subsequently, a hierarchical CFA representation [@Schmid.Leiman.1957; @Yung.etal.1999; @Brunner.etal.2012].  Although attractive, the bifactor model is not a panacea.  If the proportionality constraint does not hold, then there is no sound latent correlation to capture between-cluster relations, and inference from the fitted latent correlation is not warranted.

## Future Directions

Two future directions follow. First, it would be useful to develop formal methods for testing alignment-ratio invariance under sampling variability. One approach is to build test statistics in the spirit of vanishing tetrads and related constraint tests in factor analysis and SEM [@Wishart.1928; @Bollen.Ting.1993; @Drton.etal.2007], complemented by bootstrap procedures that assess the stability of invariance across resamples. Second, the alignment-ratio framework suggests a constructive route to cluster discovery. One could search for cluster partitions that yield approximately invariant alignment ratios, so that the resulting oblique factor structure admits a sound latent correlation. This could be implemented with combinatorial optimization. For example, one could minimize a dispersion measure of the alignment ratios (or a direct invariance criterion) over candidate partitions.
